exemplar.title: What is the pattern of distribution and accessibility of Lublin City Bikes, and what are potential factors affecting it?

detailedExemplarData.title: What is the pattern of distribution and accessibility of Lublin City Bikes, and what are potential factors affecting it?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is explicitly geographical with three clear, testable hypotheses.
Precise location (Lublin) defined with latitudeâlongitude, population, districts and relief details.
Comprehensive mapping â all maps include scale, north arrow, keys and district boundaries.
Strong theoretical linkage to Option E syllabus and urban leisure concepts.
Multiple original maps (distribution, relief) that follow cartographic conventions.
weaknesses: Introduction could deepen theoretical framing by explicitly referencing urban transport models.
Administrative relief description lacks consistent elevation units.
Justification for one hypothesis could cite spatial interaction theory more explicitly.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student meets all top-band descriptors: a clear geographical question with testable hypotheses, a precise and justified field site, and excellent mapping conventions. Minor improvements could enrich theoretical context, but overall performance warrants full marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods are described with timing, sample size (76 responses) and clear links to each hypothesis.
Selection criteria for high-elevation districts are logically justified.
Observation protocol is well explained, linking qualitative notes to research aims.
Use of app data for bike counts is clearly implemented across districts.
weaknesses: Theoretical alignment (e.g., rationale for bikes per 1 000 inhabitants) could be more fully developed.
Sampling method and demographic breakdown for the survey are not specified.
Precise timestamps for app downloads are missing, which affects reproducibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although originally assessed in band 2, the methods section provides a breadth of techniques with clear implementation, justification of district selection and solid alignment to research aims. On balance, this warrants the top band score to reflect its completeness and coherence.

strengths: A wide range of presentation techniques (tables, bar charts, pie charts, choropleth maps) enhance data interpretation.
Most data directly address the hypotheses and are sufficient for in-depth analysis.
Dynamic visualizations illustrate spatial-temporal patterns effectively.
Captions, keys and figure numbers are clear and correctly placed.
weaknesses: Occasional typographical errors in units and number formatting.
Table 2 lacks explicit units in the heading.
Bike-availability map is missing a legend for category ranges.
Table 1 title and layout could be refined for clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The information is well-treated with mostly effective presentation methods and strong relevance to the research questions. Occasional convention errors prevent a top-band award, placing this work solidly in the 3-4 range at the upper end.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Appropriate descriptive statistics (averages, bikes per 1 000 inhabitants) are calculated and compared spatially.
Interpretation identifies key trends and anomalies (e.g., CBD clustering, relief influences).
Discussion links patterns back to hypotheses and offers context for anomalies.
Analysis covers all hypotheses with coherent narrative flow.
weaknesses: No inferential statistics or confidence intervals are applied.
Calculation procedures and formulas are not fully explained.
Theoretical linkage (e.g., spatial interaction models) could be deeper.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis goes beyond basic description, identifying anomalies and linking them to geographic factors. However, absence of statistical tests and some gaps in evidence keep it in the mid-upper band (3-4), meriting a 4.

strengths: Conclusion is clear, structured and directly addresses each hypothesis.
Findings are fully supported by the preceding analysis.
The summary logically integrates evidence and highlights partial confirmations where appropriate.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion succinctly summarizes results for all hypotheses and is fully grounded in the analysis, fulfilling the top-band criteria for Criterion E.

strengths: Evaluation identifies relevant strengths and limitations of each method.
Improvement suggestions (larger sample, more districts, API access) are appropriate and linked to identified limitations.
Discussion shows awareness of validity and reliability concerns.
weaknesses: The impact of suggested improvements on validity/reliability is not fully explored.
Evaluation lacks a comprehensive explanation of how specific changes would alter results outcomes.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student outlines pertinent strengths and weaknesses and proposes relevant improvements, matching the band-2 descriptor for a clear but not exhaustive evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: Several original maps include scale, north arrow, key and appropriate labelling (e.g. distribution of stations, relief, bikes / 1000 people).  Minor aesthetic issues exist but all essential cartographic conventions are followed, so the highest band is warranted.

The fieldwork question is explicitly geographical (spatial distribution and accessibility) and is tightly linked to option E syllabus concepts of transport and urban leisure.  Three clear, testable hypotheses are stated, showing the student has moved beyond a simple descriptive aim.  This satisfies the descriptor for the top band.

A range of appropriate techniquesâtables, bar charts, pie charts and choropleth mapsâare employed and aid interpretation.  While not always the âmost effectiveâ (e.g., no statistical graphs), they clearly exceed the minimum appropriate threshold, so a high score within the 3-4 band is justified.

Techniques (counting bikes via the app, 76-response online survey, field observations over two days) are correctly implemented and yield usable data, but the sample is modest and observations do not cover every district, limiting reliability.  This corresponds to âtechniques correctly used but with minimal data collectionâ in band 2.

The student calculates averages and bikes / 1000 indicators and compares them spatially, which is an appropriate descriptive technique.  However, no inferential statistics (e.g., Spearman, ÏÂ²) or confidence levels are applied, so the work sits comfortably in the 3-4 band, lower half.

Trends (greater station density near CBD, mixed effect of relief) are described and linked back to each hypothesis with some discussion of anomalies such as Zemborzyce.  Nevertheless, the links to theory (e.g., spatial interaction) are not comprehensive.  This merits a mid-band score of 3-4.

Lublin is located with latitudeâlongitude, population size, administrative districts and physical relief all specified.  Primary data collection across the whole city is shown to be feasible, and justification for choosing the site (accessibility, data availability) is given.  This meets the âprecise location with clear feasibilityâ level.

The student identifies several pertinent strengths and limitations for each method, explaining briefly how these affect results.  The discussion is appropriate but not exhaustive or deeply analytical, placing it in the middle band.

Most data (bike counts by district, survey responses, relief maps) are directly relevant and generally sufficient to address the three hypotheses, though gaps exist in temporal coverage and sample representativeness.  This aligns with the upper level of the 3-4 band.

A clear, structured conclusion summarises findings for each hypothesis and is supported by the earlier analysis, even noting partial confirmations.  The logical link to evidence is evident, so the top band for Criterion E is earned.

Analysis covers all hypotheses but depth is uneven: limited temporal data and absence of statistical testing leave noticeable gaps.  Thus the analysis is âwith some gaps in evidenceâ, corresponding to band 3-4, lower end.

Methods (survey, observation, app download) are described with timings, sample size and some justification of survey questions, demonstrating clear relevance to each hypothesis.  However, explanations remain fairly brief and theoretical alignment (e.g. why bikes / 1000 chosen) is only partly developed, so the work fits the middle band rather than the comprehensive top band.

Captions, figure numbers, keys, scale and north arrow are mostly correct, but there are occasional typographical errors and inconsistent units (e.g., commas vs points in numbers).  This matches âoccasional errorsâ in the 3-4 band; a mid-band score is appropriate.

Improvement ideas (larger sample, more observation districts, additional survey questions, more detailed app data) are relevant and linked to identified limitations, though the likely impact on validity or reliability is not fully explored.  This matches the descriptor for band 2.

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question is explicitly geographical with clear testable components, facilitating hypothesis formation and targeted investigation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The introductory context outlines the global emergence of bike-sharing but would benefit from explicit linkage to urban transport theory to deepen geographical relevance.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 1 effectively locates Lublin within Poland and follows cartographic conventions (north arrow, scale), enhancing spatial context.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The administrative relief description lacks units for elevation ranges; specify meters above sea level consistently for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypothesis 1 is clearly worded and linked to spatial factors around the CBD; consider explicitly referencing spatial interaction models.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The distribution map (Figure 3) includes a clear legend and district boundaries, enabling effective spatial comparison of station locations.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The relief map (Figure 4) effectively represents topography with shading and district boundaries; conventions are consistently applied.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Interpretation identifies anomalies (e.g. WÄglin PoÅudniowy vs SÅawin) and links these to topographical relief, showing analytical depth.
Consider quantifying the influence of listed factors (infrastructure development, socio-economics) with supplementary data to strengthen your argument.
The observation analysis paragraph clearly describes distribution disparities, effectively linking field notes to spatial patterns.
The bike-availability map lacks a legend for the category ranges; add a clear key to improve interpretability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Justification for Hypothesis 2 would be strengthened by citing relevant urban morphology or slope-resistance theory rather than general assumptions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The syllabus link to Option E is concise and demonstrates clear theoretical relevance to leisure and urban tourism concepts.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Method description would benefit from deeper theoretical justificationâfor example, explain why survey timing and district division specifically address each hypothesis.
Survey section outlines question topics but lacks detail on sampling method and demographic breakdown; specify how representativeness was ensured.
Table 1 title spans too broadlyârefine to two columns and ensure answer categories are visually linked to purposes.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The symbol for the âNoâ option in the survey table is incorrect; replace \bigcup with a clear checkbox or textual option marker.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Observation description clearly links data collected to research aims, demonstrating sound implementation of qualitative field techniques.
When using app data, include precise timestamps for each download to ensure reproducibility and improve data reliability.
The average number of stations per district is given but the calculation procedure is not explained; include the formula or workflow used.
Selection criteria for high-elevation districts are logically justified and enhance the analytical rigour of the elevation-based spatial comparison.
Table 2 lacks units for the average station benchmark; include the exact average figure and units (stations/district) in the heading.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Visual representation of survey results (Figure 8) enhances clarity; consider adding error bars or confidence intervals for robust interpretation.
Discussion of seasonal bias in survey uptake is valuable; integrate local weather or usage data to quantify its effect.
The formula for bikes per 1000 inhabitants should be formatted as $$\frac{\text{bikes in district}}{\text{population of district}}\times1000$$ with proper fraction notation.
The dynamic visualization of bike availability over time illustrates spatial-temporal patterns effectively.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The conclusion succinctly reiterates findings linked to each hypothesis, fully supported by the preceding analysis.
Improvement suggestions (larger sample, more districts) are relevant but would gain impact by explaining how each change enhances validity or reliability.
The evaluation correctly identifies data-source limitations in the app but could propose targeted improvements (e.g., API access) to expand data quality.
